question: What evidence supports preemptive TEVAR for uncomplicated acute or subacute type B aortic dissection?
source_access_type: Original publisher PDF held in private local source workspace
relevant_sections: Secondary End Points and Adverse Events

study_characteristics.design: Prospective multicenter randomized controlled trial; intention-to-treat analysis
study_characteristics.population: 140 clinically stable patients with uncomplicated type B aortic dissection, 2 to 52 weeks after onset
study_characteristics.setting: Seven centers in Germany, Italy, and France
study_characteristics.intervention: Optimal medical therapy plus elective TEVAR (n=72 randomized)

extracted_claims.claim_text: INSTEAD randomized 140 stable patients 2 to 52 weeks after uncomplicated type B aortic dissection to optimal medical therapy with or without elective TEVAR.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: 140 patients were randomly subjected to elective stent-graft placement
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Timing range is stated in Study Population on printed p. 2520.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; not specialist reviewed

extracted_claims.claim_text: At 2 years, the trial did not show a statistically significant all-cause survival difference between TEVAR plus medical therapy and medical therapy alone.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Comparison between curves revealed no significant difference
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Survival estimates and P value appear in the abstract and Primary Outcome section.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; not specialist reviewed

extracted_claims.claim_text: The 2-year combined endpoint of aorta-related death, progression-related crossover or conversion, and ancillary procedures did not differ significantly between groups.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: There was no difference between groups
extracted_claims.section: Secondary End Points and Adverse Events
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Figure legend defines the composite and reports log-rank P=0.65.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; not specialist reviewed

extracted_claims.claim_text: Complete thoracic false-lumen thrombosis with morphological remodeling at 2 years was more frequent after TEVAR than with medical therapy alone.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: 91.3% complete false-lumen thrombosis and morphological evidence of aortic remodeling
extracted_claims.source_location_note: The comparison value for medical therapy is 19.4% in the abstract/table.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; not specialist reviewed

extracted_claims.claim_text: TEVAR carried early procedural and neurological harms, including two 30-day deaths and three neurological complications in the treated group.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Periprocedural outcomes (30 days) included 3 vascular injuries
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Table 3 reports 2 deaths, 2 paraplegia/paraparesis events, and 1 major stroke.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; not specialist reviewed

outcome_data.result_text: Three events in the TEVAR group versus one paraparesis with medical therapy
outcome_data.page_or_location: Printed p. 2519; PDF p. 1; abstract; procedural detail on printed p. 2522

limitations: The trial did not reach the assumed number of deaths and was underpowered for its primary endpoint.
The enrolled population spanned 2 to 52 weeks after onset, creating heterogeneous timing/risk.
Four enrolled patients were described as protocol violations.
The trial evaluated a specific earlier-generation Talent device and experienced centers.
contradictions: Favorable remodeling did not translate into a demonstrated 2-year survival or composite clinical-event benefit.
reviewer_notes: Original-PDF extraction only. No claim, quotation, or outcome has undergone citation verification or specialist validation.
